---
title: "Intro to Transcriptomics"
author: Nicole Black, Wade Boohar, Kayla Xu
updated by: Christopher H Kim
date: 07/17/22
updated: 03/01/26
---

###### This assignment has two main parts:
###### PART 1 will be a guided tutorial on loading and inspecting transcriptomic data fromTCGA, as well as extracting and saving information of interest (namely clinical, gene, and expression data). 
###### PART 2 will involve differential expression analysis utilizing the data you generate from Part 1.

***Deliverables***
-Upload this R Notebook to your GitHub and submit the link to your Repo on Brightspace.
-Include any graphs or figures created in this assignment in the folder with your R notebook with descriptive file names.

Since this is an optional partner activity, it is okay if your answers are the same as your partner’s as long as everyone understands it and could explain it in their own words if asked. Each person must individually push their code to Github. *At the top of your R Notebook, write the name of you and your partner(s) as a comment.*

***Complete the following coding activity and answer any following questions as comments in your R Notebook***

=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=

##### PART 1

Set your working directory
(run getwd() in the console -- the small window below the main script area -- to check that the working directory is analysis_data)
(if getwd() prints the wrong directory, run setwd("PATH TO anaylysis_data"))
```{r setup}
      knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath("/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data")) 
```

Download and load packages
```{r Package Setup}
# install, only if needed, and load packages (SummarizedExperiment and TCGAbiolinks)
if (!require("SummarizedExperiment", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("SummarizedExperiment")
  BiocManager::install("EnhancedVolcano")

library(TCGAbiolinks)
library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(EnhancedVolcano)
```

Load in the RNAseq data
```{r}
rna_query <- GDCquery(project ="TCGA-BRCA",
                      data.category = "Transcriptome Profiling",
                      data.type = "Gene Expression Quantification",
                      workflow.type = "STAR - Counts")

rna_se <- GDCprepare(rna_query)
```


###### (1) Summarized Experiment Basics

Let's take a look at rna_se (RNA Summarized Experiment). Call head() and str(). Also explore the data frame by double clicking on it from the Environment panel (or calling "View(rna_se)").

```{r Exercise 1.1}
# write code here
head(rna_se)
str(rna_se)
View(rna_se)
```

Use the dim() function to find the dimensions of the colData, rowRanges@elementMetadata, and unstranded dataframes. Record them below:

  ~ colData (rows, cols): 1231, 94
  ~ rowRanges@elementMetadata: 60660, 10
  ~ rna_se@assays@data$unstranded: 60660, 1231

```{r Exercise 1.2}
# write code here
dim(rna_se@colData)
dim(rna_se@rowRanges@elementMetadata)
dim(rna_se@assays@data$unstranded)

```

Fill out a description of the rows/columns of our data frames. You can use this as a cheat sheet moving forward.
  ~ colData
        - Rows: Individual different RNA seq samples
        - Columns: different information about each RNA seq sample (barcode,patient ID, tumor stage)
  ~ rowRanges@elementMetadata
        - Rows: individual genes
        - Columns: information about each gene (annotations made)
  ~ unstranded (in assays)
        - Rows: individual genes
        - Columns: number of samples that RNA seq has been done including the specific gene
  
```{r Exercise 1.3}
# colData: 
rownames(rna_se@colData)
colnames(rna_se@colData)

# rowRanges:
rownames(rna_se@rowRanges@elementMetadata)
colnames(rna_se@rowRanges@elementMetadata)

# what are the rows...?

# unstranded: 
rownames(rna_se@assays@data$unstranded)
colnames(rna_se@assays@data$unstranded)

# what are these?
# Hint: Do the rows/cols match dimensions of other dataframes?

```

###### (2) ColData - clinical information

For PART 2 of this week's assignment, you will be performing differential expression analysis, which calls for covariates to be controlled for. One of these covariates will be the age of patients. Let's begin inspecting the *age_at_index* column in the colData dataframe by looking at the first 15 values in it.

```{r Exercise 2.1}
# write code here
rna_se@colData$age_at_index[1:15]

```

Call the *summary()* function on the *age_at_index* column. Record the output below:
    Min.: 26.00 
    1st Qu.: 48.00
    Median: 58.00
    Mean: 58.28
    3rd Qu.: 67.00
    Max.: 89.00
    NA's: 1
    
```{r Exercise 2.2}
summary(rna_se@colData$age_at_index)

```

We see that there is one patient with an N/A value in the *age_at_index* column. We will subset this patient out later, but first we'll make our own copy of the clinical data frame that we can manipulate to our needs. Create a data frame called *rna_clinical* that contains data from colData.

```{r Exercise 2.3}
rna_clinical <- rna_se@colData

```

Use the *as.data.frame()* function to change this from an S4 object to a data frame object.

```{r Exercise 2.4}

rna_clinical <- as.data.frame(rna_clinical)
class(rna_clinical)

```

Four columns in *rna_clinical* contain nested lists. To save the dataframe as a csv later, we need to first remove those columns. Using boolean masks, subset out *treatments*, *primary_site*, *disease_type*, and *sites_of_involvement*.

```{r Exercise 2.5}

colnames_mask <- !(colnames(rna_clinical) %in% c("treatments", "primary_site", "disease_type", 
                                                  "sites_of_involvement"))
#we are masking out COLUMNS here, so the comma before the mask.
rna_clinical <- rna_clinical[,colnames_mask]

```


Remember that patient with an N/A value in age_at_index? Let's subset them out.

```{r Exercise 2.6}
age_NA_mask <- !is.na(rna_clinical$age_at_index)
  
rna_clinical <- rna_clinical[age_NA_mask,]  

summary(rna_clinical$age_at_index)
```


###### (3) RowRanges - gene information

Call *head()* on *rowRanges@elementMetadata*:

```{r Exercise 3.1}
# write code here
head(rna_se@rowRanges@elementMetadata)

```

Create a data frame called *rna_genes* that contains data from *elementMetadata*.

```{r Exercise 3.2}
rna_genes <- rna_se@rowRanges@elementMetadata


```

Use the *as.data.frame()* function on *rna_genes* to change it from an S4 object to a data frame object.

```{r Exercise 3.3}
rna_genes <- as.data.frame(rna_se@rowRanges@elementMetadata)
class(rna_genes)
```

###### (4) Assays - RNA expression data

What are the names of the 6 assays?
1. unstranded
2.stranded_first
3. stranded_second
4.  tpm_unstrand
5. fpkm_unstrand
6. fpkm_uq_unstrand

```{r Exercise 4.1}
# access the assays here
rna_se@assays@data

```

Create a new data frame called "rna_counts" that contains the data from *unstranded*.
```{r Exercise 4.2}

rna_counts <- rna_se@assays@data$unstranded
class(rna_counts)
rna_counts <- as.data.frame(rna_counts)
class(rna_counts)
```


Recall the patient with NA age data? We need to exclude that patient. We can use the same mask to do this.

Notice how in this case, we apply the mask to the columns of the *rna_counts* data frame, unlike how it was applied to the rows in the *rna_clinical* data frame.

This is because the two data frames align in a unique way: the rows of *rna_clinical* match up with the columns of *rna_counts*. These are where the sample/patients are situated in either data frames.

```{r Exercise 4.3}
rna_counts <- rna_counts[,age_NA_mask]
```

Inspect the data for the first 5 patients (columns 1-5) and the first 15 genes (rows 1-15).

```{r Exercise 4.3}
rna_counts[1:15, 1:5]

```


###### (5) Cleaning up your 3 data frames

Call *rownames()* and *colnames()* on *rna_clinical*.

```{r Exercise 5.1}
rownames(rna_clinical)
colnames(rna_clinical)
```

Just like we did to "clinical", we are going to create a column in *rna_clinical* named *age_category* to split apart younger and older patients. Our cutoff will be "young" as <= 58 y/o, and "old" as > 58 y/o.

In *rna_clinical*, there are three columns containing information on age. We'll use the column *age_at_index* (from which we just removed an N/A value from), because it had the fewest number of NA values (only 1).

Age columns:
  rna_clinical$age_at_diagnosis (16 NAs) (in days, not years)
  rna_clinical$age_at_index (1 NA) 
  rna_clinical$paper_age_at_initial_pathologic_diagnosis (19 NAs)
  

Use the information in *age_at_index* with an *ifelse()* statement to create a new column in *rna_clinical* called *age_category*.
Remember: ifelse(expression, x, y), where x happens if the expression is true, and y happens if it is false.


Just like we did to "clinical", we are going to create a column in *rna_clinical* for *age_category*. Our cutoff will be "young" as <= 58 y/o, and "old" as > 58 y/o.

```{r Exercise 5.4}
rna_clinical$age_category <- ifelse(rna_clinical$age_at_index <= 58, "young", "old")
```

----

Let's move on to *rna_genes*. Call *rownames()* and *colnames()* on *rna_genes*.

```{r Exercise 5.5}
rownames(rna_genes)
colnames(rna_genes)
```

Oh no! These row names don't look very informative. We know that the rows in *rna_genes* symbolize genes, so what should we set *rownames()* to? One of the following commands will give an error.

```{r Exercise 5.6}

rownames(rna_genes) <- rna_genes$gene_id

```

We will be using *gene_id*, since *gene_name* can contain repeats. *gene_id* entries should all be unique.
----

Now that *rna_genes* has informative row/column names, let's move on to *rna_counts.* Call *rownames()* and *colnames()* on *rna_counts*.

```{r Exercise 5.7}
rownames(rna_counts)
colnames(rna_counts)
```

Uh oh! Looks like neither the row nor column names are very informative. Given what we did to the other data frames, add column and row names to *rna_counts*. Name the columns based on the patient barcodes (ie the row names for *rna_clinical*), and name the rows based on the gene id's (ie the row names for *rna_genes*).

```{r Exercise 5.8}

rownames(rna_counts) <- rownames(rna_genes)
colnames(rna_counts) <- rownames(rna_clinical)
rownames(rna_counts)
colnames(rna_counts) 
```

Now, if we look at the data, we'll see the row names set as the gene IDs and the column names as the patient sample barcodes.

```{r Exercise 5.9}

rownames(rna_counts)
colnames(rna_counts)

```

----

Great! Now all of our data frames are ready for exploration and analysis.

  
###### (6) Saving data as csv

Loading data from TCGA can be time consuming. It's much faster to access csv files.

Save *rna_clinical* to your *analysis_data* directory. Make sure your working directory is set properly.

```{r Exercise 6.1}
write.csv(rna_clinical, "brca_rna_clinical_data.csv", row.names = TRUE)
```


Save *rna_genes* to your *analysis_data* directory.

```{r Exercise 6.2}
write.csv(rna_genes, "brca_rna_gene_data.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```


Save *rna_counts* to your *analysis_data* directory (warning: this will likely take a while!)

```{r Exercise 6.3}
write.csv(rna_counts, "brca_rna_count_data.csv", row.names = TRUE)
```


###### PART 2

In Part 1, you learned how to manipulate the SummarizedExperiment data structure and turn it into more readable dataframes, saving them as rna_counts, rna_clinical, and rna_genes. In part 2, you will use these dataframes to perform differential expression analysis based on tumor status.

*Pre-Assignment*
Once again, use the knitr function to set your working directory to your analysis_data folder in 490_cluster.
(run getwd() in the console -- the small window below the main script area -- to check that the working directory is analysis_data)
(if getwd() prints the wrong directory, run setwd("PATH TO anaylysis_data"))
```{r setup}

knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath("/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data")) 
```

If DESeq2 is not already installed, install it now
```{r}
if (!require("DESeq2", quietly = TRUE))
BiocManager::install("DESeq2")
library(DESeq2)
```

*1*
Read in the rna_clinical, rna_genes, and rna_counts dataframes which you made in Part 1.

```{r}

rna_clinical = read.csv("brca_rna_clinical_data.csv", row.names = 1)
rna_genes = read.csv("brca_rna_gene_data.csv")
rna_counts = read.csv("brca_rna_count_data.csv", row.names = 1)


```


*2*
In this assignment, you will run differential expression analysis comparing patient samples by whether the sample is from a tumor or normal tissue (this is the definition column in rna_clinical). You will need to choose a variable to control for covariance of: age and/or PAM50 subtype (paper_BRCA_Subtype_PAM50). 

Manipulate those columns so that they are ready for differential expression analysis (hint: what kind of variables are they? what data type are they by default? do you need to handle unknown values?) Filter out genes with a total expression across all patients less than 1000.
```{r}

# convert variables to factors
rna_clinical$definition <- factor(rna_clinical$definition)
rna_clinical$age_category <- factor(ifelse(rna_clinical$age_at_index <= 58, "young", "old"))

totalGenes <- rowSums(rna_counts)
geneMask <- totalGenes >= 1000

rna_counts <- rna_counts[geneMask, ]
rna_genes <- rna_genes[geneMask, ]

colnames(rna_counts)
```


*3*
Perform the differential expression analysis, All you need to do is fill in the appropriate # terms.

IMPORTANT: save the *results* data frame in your *analysis_data* directory using the last two commands in the chunk below. We will be using this for future assignments.
```{r}
colnames(rna_counts) <- gsub("\\.", "-", colnames(rna_counts))
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = rna_counts,
  colData = rna_clinical,
  design = ~ age_category + definition
)

dds_obj <- DESeq(dds)
# RUN THIS CHUNKKKKKKKKKKKKKKK run library codes before tho
resultsNames(dds_obj)

results <- results(dds_obj, format = "DataFrame", contrast = c("definition", "Primary.solid.Tumor", "Solid.Tissue.Normal"))

results <- data.frame(results)

### save in .csv and .rds

write.csv(results, "brca_DESeq_results.csv", row.names = T)
# read in later using: results = read.csv("brca_DESeq_results.csv", row.names = 1)

saveRDS(results, "brca_DESeq_results.rds")
# read in later using: results = readRDS("brca_DESeq_results.rds")
```

Prepare results dataframe for EnhancedVolcano plotting. Add two columns, "-log10(padj)" and "gene_name". Fill in these columns appropriately.
```{r}
results$gene_name <- rna_genes$gene_name
```

*4*
Now we will use the EnhancedVolcano package to plot our results. The code is already completed and should run without adjustment if all code up to here is correct.
```{r}
EnhancedVolcano(results,
                lab = results$gene_name,
                x = 'log2FoldChange',
                y = 'padj',
                title = 'Sample Definition: Tumor vs Normal Tissue',
                labSize = 3.0,
  pointSize = 1.0
              )
```

*5*
# Explain what genes from each part of the Volcano Plot mean in terms of their significance and up/down regulation. 
top-right genes: Relevant to other genes, these are significantly upregulated in the tissues because they have a large positive log2foldchange and small p-values, indicating that this huge difference is statistically significant. We have sufficient evidence to support the fact that these genes are expressed more in tumor samples than in normal tissue.

bottom-right genes: While the volcano plot also shows an upregulation in tumor tissues, because of their higher p values (from the large positive log2 fold change values), this upregulation is not statistically significant. 

top-left genes: These genes are significantly downregulated in tumor tissues, and because they have large negative log2fold chagnes and small pvalues, we have sufficient evidence that tissue type affects the downregulation of these genes in tumor tissues.

bottom-left genes: These genes are statistically significant but they have little to no fold change. From the volcano plot, it appears that the expression difference between tumor tissue and normal tissue is small, and becaue the p value is small, we have sufficient evidence that tissue type has no effect on the expresison of the genes in this region.

top-middle genes: These genesa re statistically significant (small p value) but have little to no fold change. Their expression difference among the different tissue types is small.

bottom-middle genes: These genes show little fold change are are not statistically significant. As such these genes likely have similar expression levesl in tumor and normal tissue.

Save the picture of the volcano plot (using either ggsave() or right clicking and manually downloading the image and push this .Rmd and the image to GitHub)
